coredata.dc:description: Cancer remains a major global health challenge, driving extensive research into developing advanced nano systems for precise and effective drug delivery. Carbon-based nanomaterials, particularly carbon nanotubes and quantum dots, have gained prominence due to their exceptional structural and physicochemical properties. These nanomaterials offer significant advantages, including high drug-loading capacity, enhanced biocompatibility, and reduced immunogenicity, making them highly promising candidates for cancer therapy. This review provides a detailed examination of the synthesis and functionalization of carbon nanostructures, highlighting recent advancements in their use for cancer drug delivery. Also, it analyzes their potential to improve therapeutic outcomes through targeted delivery mechanisms while addressing the challenges associated with their clinical translation. Furthermore, the review explores the prospects, challenges, and future directions of carbon nanotubes and quantum dots, focusing on their integration into clinical applications for cancer treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cancer remains a major global health challenge, driving extensive research into developing advanced nano systems for precise and effective drug delivery. Carbon-based nanomaterials, particularly carbon nanotubes and quantum dots, have gained prominence due to their exceptional structural and physicochemical properties. These nanomaterials offer significant advantages, including high drug-loading capacity, enhanced biocompatibility, and reduced immunogenicity, making them highly promising candidates for cancer therapy. This review provides a detailed examination of the synthesis and functionalization of carbon nanostructures, highlighting recent advancements in their use for cancer drug delivery. Also, it analyzes their potential to improve therapeutic outcomes through targeted delivery mechanisms while addressing the challenges associated with their clinical translation. Furthermore, the review explores the prospects, challenges, and future directions of carbon nanotubes and quantum dots, focusing on their integration into clinical applications for cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent advances in material science have significantly contributed to the growth of drug development research, particularly in cancer therapy [1,2]. Projections indicate a 400 % increase in cancer survival rates from 1977 to 2022, primarily driven by advancements in long-term survival [3]. However, the adaptability and heterogeneity of cancer cells often lead to drug resistance, reducing therapeutic efficacy [4]. Traditional methods of using anticancer drugs can severely harm healthy somatic cells, resulting in serious side effects such as baldness, pale skin, and vomiting [5–7]. Enhancing the targeting efficiency of anticancer drugs using novel nanomaterials is a primary focus of current research, aiming to reduce drug dosages and associated side effects while improving patient quality of life [8–10]. The versatility of carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanodots, graphene, carbon nanotubes, mesoporous carbon, nanodiamonds, and so on, lies in their unique structural dimensions and physicochemical properties [11–19]. These materials provide substantial drug-loading capacity, enhanced biocompatibility, and reduced immunogenicity, making them suitable for biomedical applications [20,21].

Carbon-based quantum dots (QDs) have emerged as a versatile class of nanomaterials with broad implications across various scientific fields, including chemistry, engineering, biology, and medicine [3,22,23]. The prospective applications of these nanomaterials in electronics, sensors, polymers, and imaging have generated considerable interest [24–26]. Their application in the medical field is fascinating, as they serve as platforms for attaching receptors and chemotherapy drugs, owing to their surface functional groups, such as carboxylic and amino groups [27–30]. QDs have demonstrated promising results in inhibiting cancer cell growth in vitro, including liver cancer (Hep G2) and breast cancer (MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231) cells, primarily through the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [31–34]. Additionally, QDs have shown potential for bypassing the blood-brain barrier, offering hope for treating brain-related diseases [35,36].

Another class of carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) with significant potential in drug delivery systems is carbon nanotubes (CNTs), which Iijima discovered in 1991 [25]. Like QDs, CNTs are nontoxic, possess excellent optical properties, and can be functionalized to control drug release accurately [37]. Considerable research has been conducted on their use in drug delivery, benefiting from well-understood release mechanisms facilitated by the acidic environment of tumors [30,38–40]. Drug delivery and dye degradation are also highly efficient in a green, dual-functional nano-hybrid system utilizing graphene oxide-decorated diatom silica and polydopamine-coated diatoms [41,42]. While significant progress has been made, ongoing research is needed to address existing challenges and explore new possibilities for anticancer drug conjugates and targeted delivery systems. The potential of CBNs in improving patient outcomes in cancer therapy remains substantial, offering promising avenues for future research and clinical applications. This review summarizes carbon-based nanostructure synthesis, functionalization, cancer therapy, and drug delivery applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon-based quantum dots and nanotubes for drug delivery applications
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of carbon-based nanostructures employs various methods, each designed to create specific types of nanostructures with desired properties. The following outlines the fabrication methods for carbon-based quantum dots and nanotubes.

Today, carbon-based QDs are produced either from macroscopic carbonaceous materials such as activated carbon, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and graphite using top-down processes or from small molecules under high temperature and pressure using bottom-up approaches [43–46]. Each method has advantages and disadvantages, and the choice of precursors influences the properties of the resulting carbon-based QDs, such as size, distribution, and surface functionalization. However, top-down techniques typically require high energy, high potential, and highly acidic conditions, making them more challenging than bottom-up processes. For drug delivery applications, the bottom-up approach is generally preferred over the top-down approach for synthesizing QDs due to better control over particle size, surface functionality, and biocompatibility. The techniques for fabricating carbon-based QDs are shown in Fig. 1 .

Top-down approaches can produce highly crystalline QDs, but controlling particle size and surface chemistry is more challenging. This makes most QDs produced through top-down approaches unsuitable for drug delivery applications. While the bottom-up approach is generally preferred for drug delivery, some top-down approaches can still be used effectively if optimized to ensure biocompatibility and minimal toxicity. Electrochemically synthesized QDs can be functionalized with biopolymers (e.g., PEG and chitosan) to improve drug loading and reduce cytotoxicity. Additionally, negatively charged groups can be introduced into QDs, increasing their hydrophilicity and making them ideal for drug delivery.

In bottom-up approaches, QDs are produced from molecular precursors (e.g., citric acid, glucose, and amino acids) through hydrothermal or microwave-assisted methods. In the microwave-assisted method, microwave radiation is used to rapidly heat and decompose molecular precursors (e.g., citric acid, glucose, and urea) in a solvent medium. The localized high-temperature environment promotes carbonization, nucleation, and self-assembly of carbon nanoparticles, forming fluorescent QDs [54]. This method is energy-efficient, scalable, and environmentally friendly, producing CDs with tunable size, high water solubility, and surface functionalization for applications such as drug delivery, bioimaging, and sensing. The hydrothermal method involves heating organic precursors under high pressure in a sealed hydrothermal reactor [55]. This process is highly versatile and may utilize various raw materials, including proteins, chitosan, citric acid, and glucose, to produce carbon-based QDs.

Hydrothermal and microwave-assisted synthesis methods for drug delivery applications are generally preferred for producing QDs due to their advantages in biocompatibility, high quantum yield, and ease of surface functionalization. The hydrothermal method allows easy doping with heteroatoms (e.g., N, S, and P) to enhance drug-loading capacity. One example of hydrothermal treatment involves using sugar cane juice as a carbon source [56]. In this process, sugar cane juice and ethanol are enclosed within an autoclave reactor for 180 min at 120 °C, changing color from colorless to brown. Sun et al. [57] developed a hydrothermal method to fabricate N- and S-co-doped carbon-based QDs using gardenia fruit as the raw material.

CNTs can be produced using different techniques, including arc discharge, laser ablation, and chemical vapor deposition (CVD) methods. Arc discharge is one of the oldest methods used to synthesize CNTs. This method involves the generation of an electric arc by two electrodes when a direct current flows between them [58]. A standard setup of the arc discharge method for synthesizing CNTs is illustrated in Fig. 4 a. The procedure starts by installing the electrodes within a vacuum chamber and consistently filling them with inert gas. This inert gas aids the rapid carbon deposition on the electrodes, assisted by the plasma generated inside the chamber. The laser ablation process (Fig. 4b) utilizes a pulsed laser to ablate a carbon target in the presence of a catalyst and an inert gas, resulting in CNTs [58]. Though this method produces high-quality CNTs, it has metal catalyst impurities (e.g., Fe, Ni, and Co), which require extensive purification for biomedical applications. The CVD method is commonly used to efficiently synthesize CNTs from suitable catalysts and carbon sources [59]. Another technique, inductively coupled CVD (ICPCVD), can synthesize crystalline CNTs even at temperatures as low as 500 °C. Plasma-enhanced CVD (PECVD), which operates at lower temperatures, is crucial for microstructure synthesis and results in less contaminated nanotubes than CVD. CVD is the most suitable synthesis method for carbon nanotubes (CNTs) for drug delivery applications because it produces high-purity, well-aligned, and structurally controlled CNTs with fewer defects. It enables easy functionalization (e.g., PEGylation and biomolecule attachment) to improve biocompatibility and targeting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of carbon-based nanostructures for drug delivery applications
ce:section.ce:para: The development of specifically tailored drug delivery systems is influenced by several important factors [61]. These factors encompass the need to discover a streamlined delivery method, sustain drug efficacy, optimize drug loading, and release kinetics for specific drug targets. The distinct characteristics of carbon nanomaterials' diverse structural features have been utilized as carriers for drug delivery [62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of carbon-based quantum dots as drug delivery vehicle
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based QDs may establish covalent chemical bonds with molecules of various sizes. This unique property makes them suitable for delivering different types of drugs [62,63]. Moreover, carbon dots are very well suited for delivering multiple drugs due to their small size, low toxicity, remarkable biocompatibility, and intrinsic solubility [64]. QDs also exhibit an exceptional ability to bind to receptors and penetrate cells. Fig. 5 a depicts different QDs-based drug composites used in cancer drug delivery.

Doxorubicin (Dox) is an effective anticancer agent that binds DNA through intercalation and inhibits macromolecular biosynthesis [61,65–67]. This also hinders the advancement of topoisomerase II, an enzyme responsible for relaxing DNA supercoils for transcription. Dox prevents the release of the DNA double helix by stabilizing the topoisomerase II complex, which has already cut the DNA chain for replication [65]. This mechanism effectively halts the replication process. The production of ROS is considered a cytotoxic effect of Dox on cancer cells [68]. Dox is frequently used to treat specific lymphomas, sarcomas, and leukemia [65]. However, various reports [69,70] have indicated positive outcomes with Dox in breast cancer and brain tumors. Yang et al. [71] demonstrated that QDs-Dox composites successfully trigger death in human lung cancer cells. They used a mouse model with A549 xenograft nude tumors in their study. Moreover, the complexes demonstrated a remarkable ability to suppress the growth of tumors in comparison to the typical medication [71]. A further investigation revealed that in accordance with the first-order release kinetics principles, QDs and Dox demonstrate an ideal drug release profile at physiological and slightly acidic pH levels. Furthermore, studies have shown that receptor-mediated delivery reduces toxic effects in normal cells [72]. This mechanism can be highly effective in treating tumors in the early gastrointestinal system or in individuals with acidosis, a condition marked by lower pH levels [72].

Two studies [72,73] revealed that QDs-Dox complexes within HeLa cells underwent separation after 6 h and entered the nuclear regions of the HeLa cells after 8 h of incubation. The fluorescence of both C-dots and Dox was disrupted when they were in their conjugated states. Restoration of fluorescence was observed in both QDs and Dox upon separation of the QDs-Dox combination within the tumor cell. This restoration was visible under a confocal microscope. These findings suggest that pH-dependent release effectively releases the attached conjugate system [72]. In a specific investigation, Dong et al. [74] utilized a complex nanocarrier of arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (RGD)-conjugated graphene QDs as a fluorescent agent for delivering chemotherapy. Dox exhibited a loading efficiency of around 54.6 % on the surface of GQDs-RGD. Dox was found to be released at a rate of around 40.1 % in acidic surroundings with a pH of 5.0, whereas only some 9.2 % of Dox was released in neutral conditions with a pH of 7.4 after 72 h. According to the cell viability assays, it was found that Dox-GQDs-RGD had a higher level of toxicity in U251 human glioma cells than free Dox (Fig. 5b and c) [75].

Ding et al. [76] conducted a study in which they developed a nanocarrier theranostic agent combining GQDs with Cy5.5 (Cy) dye using a cathepsin D-responsive (P) peptide. They then loaded this nanocarrier with Dox to facilitate the controlled monitoring of anticancer drug delivery. The penetration of GQD-P-Cy into the core region of 4T1 multicellular tumor spheroid (MCTS) was investigated. The findings revealed that Dox-induced cell apoptosis in MCTS. Furthermore, the compound Dox@GQD-P-Cy demonstrated highly effective inhibition of MCTS growth following extended treatment, surpassing the effects of free Dox. The Dox@GQD-P-Cy nanocomposite demonstrated remarkable beneficial effects in both in vitro and in vivo cancer treatment due to its enhanced ability to penetrate tissues and cellular uptake. Compared to the group that received the freely administered medication, the tumor volume accounted for just 33 % of the whole volume. At 30 days, the Dox@GQD-P-Cy group showed a superior survival rate among mice with tumors compared to the Dox group, in which all mice succumbed to the tumor within 24 days (Fig. 6 ) [76].

Promising results have been observed when other antitumor drugs are conjugated with QDs. A study [77] found that QDs conjugated to paclitaxel and Hyaluronan effectively killed tumor cells. These QDs showed the capacity to image the tumor and track its progression using near-infrared light. Additionally, they released paclitaxel in a manner that depended on the pH. N-doped GQDs were produced via a hydrothermal process that included citric acid and urea. Subsequently, the N-GQDs were loaded with methotrexate (MTX), a pharmaceutical agent used to treat cancer [78]. During an in vitro study on human breast cancer cells, it was observed that free MTX rapidly diffused into and out of the cells. This led to higher cytotoxicity than MTX-N-GQDs under incubation conditions of fewer than 24 h. The extended incubation period reduced MTX-N-GQDs diffusion into and out of the cells, leading to increased cytotoxicity compared to unbound MTX.

In another investigation [79], the researchers produced GQDs through pyrolysis of citric acid. These GQDs were then used to load the anticancer drug cytarabine (Cyt), which is hydrophilic. The resulting drug was then encapsulated in chitosan (CS) gels. The fluorescent stability of GQDs-Cyt was significantly improved when wrapped with CS. This improvement can be attributed to preventing GQD agglomeration caused by the CS gels. Over 75 h, the total Cyt release was 75.6 % at pH = 5.8, 37.8 % at pH = 6.5, and 16.6 % at pH = 7.4 (Fig. 7 ). The combined Cyt release from CS/GQDs/Cyt at pH 7.4 was approximately 18.3 % of the total amount released from GQDs/Cyt. Conversely, the total amount released from GQDs/Cyt at pH 5.8 and 6.5 was significantly lower. The smaller cumulative amounts released resulted from the synergistic effect between hydrolysis and the CS gels.

Dacarbazine (DC) was incorporated into fucose-based CQDs. These CQDs were then coated with exosomes (Ex-DC@CQDs) derived from breast cancer cells [80]. Exosomes were shown to enhance the delivery of DC@CQDs to cancer cells by modulating heparan sulfate proteoglycan (HSPG) receptors. The consequence was an augmented depolarization of the mitochondrial membrane potential, ROS generation, and apoptosis activation, finally leading to cellular death (Fig. 8 ). Ex-DC@CQDs demonstrated exceptional antitumor targeting and effectiveness compared to free DC, as observed through in vivo imaging and pharmacokinetic studies. This improvement can be attributed to a more advanced pharmacokinetic profile, higher tumor accumulation through exosome-mediated HSPG receptor-driven cell uptake, and sustained release of the Ex-DC@CQDs.

Quercetin (QC), a highly effective flavonoid with anticancer properties, encounters difficulties attributed to its hydrophobic characteristics and limited solubility. Copper carbon quantum dots (Cu-CQDs) were used to enhance the solubility and stability of QC in a pH-sensitive hydrogel nanocomposite consisting of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and starch. This system aimed to enhance quality control delivery efficiency while reducing negative consequences. The nanocomposite demonstrated high drug loading and encapsulation efficiency of 47.00 % ± 0.45 and 86.25 % ± 0.75, respectively. These results are among the most impressive reported for similar systems. The cytotoxicity of CMC/starch/Cu-CQDs@QC against cancerous cells was significant, resulting in a reduction of U87-MG cell viability to 48 %. However, cytotoxicity did not significantly impact normal L929 cells, with cell viability remaining high at 92 %, resulting in targeted delivery [81]. To improve the potency and bioavailability of the QC drug, a pH-sensitive hydrogel consisting of polyacrylic acid (PAA) and PEG with CQDs dispersed within it was prepared [82]. The size and shape of the PAA-PEG-CQDs-QC nanoplatforms were controlled by synthesizing it using the double emulsion technique. This technique achieved remarkably excellent drug loading and encapsulation efficiencies of 47 % and 89 %, respectively. These high efficiencies can be attributed to the hydrogen bonding between QC, PAA, and PEG and the π-π interactions associated with CQDs.

A sustained and selective drug release was also observed under acidic conditions, resembling the tumor site [82]. The cytotoxicity of PAA, PAA-PEG, PAA-PEG-CQDs, and PAA-PEG-CQD-QC on the MCF-7 cancer cell line was evaluated using 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) salt and flow cytometry. The experimental results demonstrated that the drug-loaded nanocomposite exhibited significantly greater cytotoxicity compared to the free drug. Moreover, it induced increased apoptotic cell death. This could be attributed to the combined effect of all the components in the nanocomposite, which effectively prevented the survival of cancer cells [82].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Receptor-based delivery systems using carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In pediatric tumors, the combination of QDs conjugated to Dox and transferrin was found to induce tumor death more frequently than utilizing the free drug alone. Additionally, it was demonstrated that these conjugates can bypass the blood-brain barrier [61,83,84]. Functionalized QDs with folic acid (FA) have shown promise in identifying malignant cells that overexpress FA. These QDs tend to be endocytosed more frequently than normal cells [85,86]. In addition, integrating QDs with an anticancer drug (e.g., Dox) leads to a comparable effect but with the additional advantage of triggering the death of tumors [87]. Interestingly, this particular system utilized bovine serum albumin to promote biocompatibility and increase drug loading [87]. The utilization of hyaluronan-conjugated QDs in gene therapy and cell imaging has been substantiated by various studies, showcasing their capacity to transport both medicines and genes effectively [88]. The study utilized QDs that were synthesized from polyethylenimine. After comparing the toxicity of the initial substance and QDs, it was observed that the QDs exhibited significantly lower toxicity. The list of QDs used in cancer drug delivery is presented in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of carbon nanotubes as drug delivery vehicle
ce:section.ce:para: CNTs have unique properties that make them well-suited for drug delivery purposes. This material features distinctive tubular hollow monolithic frameworks, an ideal length-to-diameter ratio, and a large surface area. Furthermore, CNTs possess several advantageous properties. They are ultralightweight materials that exhibit excellent permeable properties across biological membranes. Additionally, multiple molecules can conjugate with them due to their versatile surface chemistry. Functionalized CNTs exhibit biocompatibility, minimal or negligible immunogenicity, and remarkable photoluminescent properties [89–91]. The hydrophilicity, solubility, and biocompatibility of CNTs are enhanced by covalently functionalizing them with various molecules, including polymers and biological molecules [62,92–94]. Additionally, CNTs can easily cross biological boundaries and enter the cytoplasm by passing through the membrane [95,96]. Researchers [62] have also suggested that CNTs may transport drugs to cells through various pathways, including energy-dependent mechanisms such as macropinocytosis, endocytosis, phagocytosis, and energy-independent mechanisms (e.g., membrane permeation). The different forms of CNTs include single-wall (SWCNT), double-wall (DWCNT), and multi-wall nanotubes (MWCNT), which vary in length, ranging from a few hundred nanometers to several microns, and in diameter, ranging from 1 to 100 nm [62]. Fig. 9 depicts different CNT drug composites used in cancer drug delivery.

For effective cancer treatment, drugs must induce substantial damage to the specific cells. To achieve this, specific chemotherapy drugs may have different mechanisms of action that are more effective on particular tumors than others [61]. Compared to conventional chemotherapy, nano-delivery vehicles can be more effective because these systems have a higher efficacy of drug delivery to tumor cells, resulting in enhanced anticancer treatment [61].

Several reports [61,97–99] have demonstrated the application of CNTs in drug delivery. The photothermal effect of PEG-functionalized SWCNTs coupled with Dox was examined by Jeyamohan et al. [100] to achieve targeted delivery and quickly eliminate breast cancer cells. According to the in vitro study, Dox binds to SWCNTs at a pH of 7.4 and is released at a lysosomal pH of 4.0, indicative of the tumor microenvironment. Findings from studies using mouse fibroblast cell lines L929 and breast cancer cell lines MCF7 showed that the combination of Dox-conjugated SWCNTs enhanced and accelerated the death of breast cancer cells [100]. Another study revealed the attachment of Fe-conjugated SWCNTs to the endoglin/CD105 antibody and Dox. In this study, 4T1 (4T1-Luc2) murine breast cancer cells were used for the biocompatibility test, which expressed luciferase (Luc2) [62,101]. Another investigation achieved a controlled release of Dox using modified SWCNTs containing alginate and chitosan. The surface of SWCNTs was modified by adsorbing Dox through π–π stacking, resulting in the formation of SWCNTs with heterogeneous surfaces. The Dox was uniformly loaded onto the surface of polysaccharide-modified SWCNTs [98].

To deliver Dox as a cancer treatment, Anaraki et al. [102] made a nanofibrous scaffold out of PLA (polylactic acid), PEG, and MWCNTs. The study demonstrated that the release of Dox remained effective for more than 30 days. Similarly, A549 lung cancer cells were treated with a nanofibrous scaffold made of Dox-loaded PLA/PEG/MWCNT conjugate for 72 h. This treatment led to an enhanced cell death rate of 65–92 %, significantly higher than the 60–62 % cell death rate observed with free Dox [102]. Jagusiak et al. [103] developed a novel drug delivery system using SWCNTs and Congo red (CR). The CR helps to create self-assembled ribbon-like structures, which are used to deliver Dox. They found that the significant presence of nanotube-bound CR considerably enhances the drug carrier's capacity. The analyzed system's high drug-binding ability and potential for pH-controlled release may lead to extended and localized drug effects.

Gemcitabine (Gem) is a nucleoside analogue. During replication, it binds to the elongating DNA strand and inhibits its progression, leading to cellular apoptosis. Gem also inhibits ribonucleotide reductase, producing deoxyribonucleotides and subsequent cell death [104]. It is frequently used to treat mesotheliomas, bladder cancers, lung cancers, pancreatic cancers, ovarian cancers, cervical cancers, and so on [105]. Gem-conjugated carbon nanoparticles have been found to inhibit metastasis and hinder tumor growth when used as a drug delivery vehicle [106–108]. A decline in lymph node metastasis was also observed by Yang et al. [108]. In addition, the study revealed that magnetic MWCNTs exhibited greater effectiveness compared to magnetically activated carbon particles. An external magnetic field can enhance nanoparticle aggregation in specific regions, increasing drug absorption in mice [109].

In a study by Razzazan et al. [110], SWCNTs were functionalized through various methods, including acylation, carboxylation, PEGylation, and amination. The SWCNTs were then conjugated with Gem. The nanostructures demonstrated a significant loading capacity. In particular, 43.14 % (w/w) of Gem was found to be bound to SWCNT-Gem conjugates, whereas 37.32 % was bound to SWCNT-PEG-Gem. The conjugated structures exhibited stability at pH below 7.4, while Gem release occurred at pH above 7.4. In vitro studies on human lung cancer (A549) and human pancreatic cancer (MIA PaCa-2) showed that cellular absorption of SWCNT-Gem and SWCNT-PEG-Gem was higher than that of the unbound drugs. This significantly improved the effectiveness of the drugs against the tumors (Fig. 10 ).

Conjugation of other antitumor drugs to CNTs has also shown promising results [111]. Using CNTs attached to paclitaxel led to enhanced tumor cell death in comparison to the administration of the drug alone. CNTs significantly improved the cellular uptake of the drug, resulting in a more than tenfold increase in intracellular paclitaxel levels. The conjugated CNT system also extended the shelf life of paclitaxel by expanding the drug's blood circulation. Remarkably, mice did not exhibit any discernible toxic effects. A separate study [112] found that cisplatin coupled with CNTs exhibited 4–6 % higher anticancer effectiveness in mice than free cisplatin. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) coated DWCNTs were successfully created that could be used for both drug and gene delivery. These DWCNTs were loaded with chloroquine, releasing the DNA cargo from the lysosomes. The functionalized DWCNTs showed minimal cytotoxicity when used at an optimal transfection concentration of 75 μg mL−1 free chloroquine in HeLa cells [62,113].

A particularly intriguing approach is the potential to attach multiple drugs to a single vehicle, enabling cooperative action. PEG-conjugated MWCNTs have demonstrated their effectiveness as a drug carrier in overcoming multi-drug resistance [61,114]. Several studies [61,115–117] have investigated the use of micelles, which are composed of PEG and PVA, for dual drug delivery. Dual-drug delivery systems provide a solution to the constraints of single-drug delivery systems by enabling independent drug release [61]. This ensures that the drug remains intact and functional.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Receptor-based delivery systems using carbon nanotubes
ce:section.ce:para: Various receptors may be overexpressed across multiple cancer cells, enabling targeted delivery of anticancer drugs through ligands that enhance tumor penetration with greater selectivity [105]. Commonly used ligands include transferrin, FA, and hyaluronan (Fig. 11 ). While several receptors can stimulate cell growth and proliferation, combining chemotherapy drugs with short half-lives, and their ability to cause DNA damage during replication may lead to synergistic effects [61]. If receptor-induced proliferation takes place after ligand endocytosis, it is anticipated that cellular apoptosis will occur due to the anticancer agent's action [61].

FA is essential in synthesizing pyrimidines and purines, which regulates cell development and division [105]. The strategy to utilize the folate receptor for targeted therapy was conceived by Leamon and Low [118]. FA and conjugated FA have been found to exhibit high specificity in binding to the folate receptor [119]. Various types of tumors, such as epithelial, ovarian, cervical, breast, lung, kidney, colorectal, and brain tumors, have been found to exhibit overexpression of the folate receptor [120,121]. A study [73] utilized MWCNTs to target cancer cells by utilizing the folate receptor. The researchers utilized confocal Raman microscopy to track cellular uptake within carcinoma cells. Multiple studies [73,86,98,122] have demonstrated the potency of folate receptor-conjugated carbon nanoparticles in drug delivery applications. Researchers [61,123] also found that when SWCNTs are combined with the folate receptor subjected to near-infrared (NIR) light (700–1100 nm), it can destroy cells. This is caused by excessive heating within the CNT, which occurs when it aggregates near regions with a high folate receptor density.

The surface of carboxylated functionalized MWCNTs was modified by incorporating FA and methotrexate (MTX) using ethylenediamine (ED), resulting in the creation of MWCNT-ED-FA and MWCNT-ED-MTX and investigating the release of MTX as a free drug in addition to MWCNT-ED-MTX [124]. Results showed that the MTX release rate from MWCNT-ED-MTX was lesser when compared to free MTX due to the conjugation of MTX on the MWCNT. The effectiveness of MWCNT-ED-FA and MWCNT-ED-MTX in combating breast cancer was assessed in the MCF-7 cell line. The cytotoxicity of MWCNT-ED-MTX is significantly higher than that of MWCNT-ED-FA, which can be attributed to the presence of MTX. In addition, the study examined the effects of MWCNT-ED-FA and MWCNT-ED-MTX on MCF-7 cells when exposed to IR laser radiation, explicitly focusing on their potential anticancer properties. The cytotoxic effects of MWCNT-ED-FA and MWCNT-ED-MTX were either similar or increased when exposed to the laser (Fig. 12 ), and the photothermal effect outweighs other stimuli, effectively concealing their impact. This phenomenon can be attributed to the absorption of laser radiation by MWCNTs, resulting in energy conversion into heat. As a result, cancer cells are effectively destroyed. FA has been found to enable targeted trials, demonstrating that MWCNT-ED-FA is effective for cancer cells.

Transferrin receptor 1 facilitates the cellular uptake of iron through clathrin-coated pits. After iron release, the receptors are recycled with apotransferrin [125]. Transferrin is particularly relevant for targeting brain tumors. It can bypass the blood-brain barrier, a common challenge other delivery vehicles encounter [126]. Dox, a transferrin-conjugated chemotherapy compound, has shown promising results in overcoming drug resistance in different types of cancer cells, such as carcinoma, leukemia, and glioblastoma [61,127–130]. This study demonstrates the significant improvement in the effectiveness of the chemotherapy agent docetaxel when combined with transferrin through CNTs in lung cancer treatment. This combination achieved a 136-fold efficiency increase compared to docetaxel alone [131].

CD44 receptors can bind to hyaluronan or hyaluronic acid, playing a crucial role as adhesion regulators. It is widely recognized that they have a significant impact on hematopoiesis and the activation of lymphocytes [132]. Tumors such as lymphomas have been observed to show increased levels of CD44 [61,132]. CD44 has also been determined to be overexpressed in breast, pancreatic, colon, intestinal, prostate, and leukemia cancers. This overexpression is attributed to CD44's function in mediating receptor tyrosine kinases within the cell [133,134]. Hyaluronan should lengthen to 6–8 units to interact with the CD44 receptor. Generally, more miniature conjugates are more effective in this interaction. It plays a crucial role in cell replication, growth, and migration. Additionally, it is often implicated in advancing certain tumors because of its wide range of downstream effectors, including Ras and phosphoinositide 3-kinase (PI-3K) [61,135–137].

The targeting efficacy of MWCNTs has been investigated utilizing hyaluronic acid (HA) [138]. The study involved the incorporation of Dox into MWCNTs and subsequent modification with polyethyleneimine (PEI). The conjugation between fluorescein isothiocyanate (FI) and HA resulted in the formation of a complex through a covalent bond. This complex was utilized for the specific targeting of cancer cells. The overexpression of CD44 receptors indicated the successful delivery of Dox to cancer cells. The application of HA-modified MWCNTs shows promise in effectively delivering drugs to cancer cells [138,139].

Liu et al. [140] have effectively produced NH2-SWCNTs coated with HA, which have shown promising results in the targeted delivery of Dox to enhance breast cancer therapy. The study found that the release of Dox was influenced by pH levels, with a faster release occurring in the acidic tumor cell microenvironment (pH below 5.5) compared to the standard conditions (pH 7) [140]. The findings indicate that pH is a key factor in enabling the release of drugs inside cells. The non-covalent coating of NH2-SWCNTs on SWCNTs-Dox-HA was examined using flow cytometry and confocal microscopy. The coating explicitly targets the CD receptor, with HA binding exclusively. There was an improvement in the intracellular delivery of Dox in CD44 overexpressing MDA-MB-231 cells [140]. The results demonstrated that treatment with SWCNTs-Dox-HA enhanced apoptosis. The list of CNTs used in cancer drug delivery is presented in Table 2 .

Carbon-based nanomaterials, particularly carbon QDs and CNTs, offer significant potential to transform cancer therapy through precise drug delivery and targeted treatment strategies. Their distinctive characteristics, such as tunable surface chemistry, high surface area, photoluminescence, and ability to penetrate biological barriers, make them highly appropriate for biomedical applications. Carbon QDs, with their excellent fluorescence properties [141], are being investigated for bioimaging and diagnostics, allowing for real-time monitoring of drug delivery and tumor development, while CNTs excel as drug carriers due to their remarkable drug-loading capacity and ability to penetrate deeply into tissues, enabling effective delivery of anticancer drugs to tumor sites. In addition to traditional chemotherapy, these materials are being integrated with innovative therapeutic approaches such as photothermal therapy [142], photodynamic therapy [142], and gene therapy [143,144]. Surface functionalization of carbon QDs and CNTs with targeting ligands, antibodies, or aptamers improves their selectivity for cancer cells, reducing off-target effects and enhancing therapeutic effectiveness. Over the past decade, CNTs have been extensively investigated for drug delivery applications, particularly following the discovery that CNTs can penetrate cells independently without causing noticeable cytotoxic effects [145,146]. The high aspect ratio of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) allows for exceptional drug-loading capacity while preserving their ability to penetrate cells effectively. Additionally, with their ultrahigh surface area, excellent mechanical properties, and electrical conductivity, CNTs serve as outstanding materials for nanoscaffolds and three-dimensional nanocomposites [145].

Despite the promising prospects, several challenges should be addressed before the widespread clinical use of carbon QDs and CNTs in cancer treatment. One of the foremost concerns is toxicity [147–150]. Firstly, pristine CNTs, due to their metallic nature, are insoluble and tend to form large bundles or ropes in various solvents, including water, which limits their immediate applicability. Secondly, CNTs lack homogeneity in their size, including both diameter and length [145]. This variability poses challenges in generating reproducible results, which are essential for evaluating biological activity in relation to specific structures. Significant efforts have been dedicated to the surface functionalization of CNTs for medicinal applications; however, limited progress has been made in ensuring their homogeneity. Developing methods to produce CNTs with uniform size is crucial for their future clinical use in treatments.

The bioconjugation of QDs may impede cell delivery, as certain materials utilized in their production may exhibit cytotoxicity [151,152]. The metabolism and excretion of QDs remain uncertain, leading to potential toxicity in the body [151,153]. The toxicology of CNTs has garnered significant attention in recent years. Purified single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), treated with acid, show no acute toxicity, unlike non-purified CNTs. Nevertheless, they have been demonstrated to induce ROS in human lung carcinoma epithelial A549 cells and NR8383 cells [145,154]. Research indicated that pristine SWNTs, whether acid-treated or not, can lead to increased chromosome and DNA damage, as well as oxidative stress in macrophage cell lines [145,155,156]. In contrast, evenly distributed CNTs with higher surface functionalization show different properties. The van der Waals forces on the surfaces of pristine CNTs lead to hydrophobic interactions, causing aggregation and network formation, subsequently contributing to prolonged toxicity.

Scalability and repeatability in producing carbon QDs and CNTs present another challenge [157]. Variability in size, morphology, and surface properties can affect their biological behavior and therapeutic outcomes, making large-scale, repeatable synthesis techniques critical for their clinical application. Additionally, regulatory constraints and the lack of standardization in toxicity assessment and quality control impede the transition of these materials from the laboratory to the clinic. Moreover, tumor heterogeneity and the intricacies of the tumor microenvironment may restrict the effectiveness of carbon-based nanocarriers. The diverse physical and metabolic characteristics of tumors make it difficult for a one-size-fits-all approach to targeting and delivering drugs effectively to all patients.

To overcome these limitations and fully realize the potential of carbon QDs and CNTs for cancer therapy, more research and development are required. The development of non-toxic QDs, including biowaste-derived carbon dots, zinc oxide QDs, graphene QDs, and other carbon dots sourced from natural materials such as milk and fruit peels, offers a promising solution to mitigate the toxicity concerns associated with traditional carbon QDs [151,152]. Functionalizing CNTs effectively addresses their aggregate tendency, reducing toxicity [145]. Therefore, future research may focus on advancing strategies to minimize toxicity while optimizing the use of CNTs and carbon dots as drug delivery systems.

Engineering carbon QDs and CNTs with biodegradable or safer surface coatings may mitigate toxicity issues, and more standardized safety methods should be established to accelerate regulatory clearance. Advances in surface modification and targeting strategies will be crucial for increasing the selectivity of carbon nanomaterials for cancer cells. Functionalizing carbon QDs and CNTs with specific tumor-targeting ligands, antibodies, or peptides tailored to individual patients' tumors will help improve targeting efficiency while minimizing off-target side effects. The future of carbon-based nanomaterials in cancer therapy lies in their integration with cutting-edge technologies such as CRISPR-based gene editing [158], immunotherapy [159], and theranostics [160]. Integrating carbon QDs and CNTs into these platforms can offer synergistic effects, opening up new possibilities for multifunctional cancer therapies. Facilitating clinical adoption necessitates optimizing regulatory routes and formulating more explicit criteria for assessing carbon nanomaterials. Collaboration between researchers, clinicians, and regulatory bodies will be necessary to bridge the gap between preclinical studies and clinical trials. Developing carbon QD- and CNT-based systems that adapt to the dynamic tumor microenvironment is a promising direction. Approaches such as employing stimuli-responsive nanomaterials that release drugs in response to pH changes, enzymes, or heat could enhance the precision of drug delivery in heterogeneous tumors.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have garnered significant attention in drug delivery, particularly in the fight against cancer. Their unique structural and physicochemical properties, including high surface area, tunable surface chemistry, and ability to penetrate biological barriers, offer distinct advantages for improving the precision and efficacy of cancer therapies. Through innovative functionalization strategies, carbon QDs and CNTs can enhance tumor selectivity, reduce off-target effects, and have the capacity to overcome challenges such as multi-drug resistance and tumor heterogeneity. Furthermore, their integration with advanced therapeutic approaches, such as photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy, and gene therapy, underscores their versatility as multifunctional platforms for cancer treatment. However, addressing the challenges of toxicity, scalability, and tumor heterogeneity will be key to realizing their full potential. Future innovations in precision functionalization, integration with emerging therapies, and regulatory frameworks will pave the way for their clinical translation, offering hope for more effective and personalized cancer treatments in the near future.
